segments.title: Warenne power politics in Defenders of the Norman Crown
segments.summary: The book review uses the Warenne earls to frame medieval aristocratic power as land, castles, marriage ties, and factional politics rather than only kings and battles.
segments.body: The discussion captures the episode's core value as public-history coverage. Dr. Clarke praises Sharon Bennett Connolly's Defenders of the Norman Crown for following a powerful but not top-tier noble family, then tracks how the Warennes' castles, marriage alliances, royal wardship, Gaveston crisis, and feud with Lancaster reveal the mechanics of medieval political power.

evidence.note: The review frames the book as a study of a wealthy, castle-holding noble family that is powerful but not among the very highest nobility.
The transcript follows the Ordinances, Gaveston's return, Scarborough surrender, and John de Warenne's role in the custody crisis.
The review connects Warenne to the Lancaster feud, Alice de Lacy abduction, castle seizures, and the loss or exchange of estates.
